sections.title: General background
sections.text: You work at a company that uses technology created by its development department to sell products on a daily basis. Every six months, your manager evaluates the efficiency of the application and checks whether it needs further service functionality.

sections.title: Current environment
sections.text:
- The company uses Microsoft 365 and Azure services.
- The application is built using Microsoft Power Platform components.
- The application contains connectors that connect it to services provided by Microsoft. These services provide a display for the products, the available delivery companies, and customer services.
- Feedback from customers is captured and stored in Azure storage using the JSON format.
- When a product order is placed, it needs approval from the department in charge before the product can be dispatched to the customer. Sometimes, the purchase order takes time to be approved.
- Your company deals with several delivery companies. These companies do not charge for delivery if the product is not delivered on time.
- The company uses Power BI to analyze data trends and to keep track of the financial health of the company.
- The company uses Azure Stream Analytics to analyze data related to products in real time.
- Azure Stream Analytics jobs have direct access to the workspace within the company's Power BI.

sections.title: Business requirements
sections.text:
Your manager has identified that the following functionality needs to be added to the application:
- The created application should connect to the services provided by the delivery companies and retrieve information from their APIs when products are delivered for free.
- When an employee in the customer service department uses the application to review a customer's orders, customer feedback data should be retrieved from Azure Storage and sent to the application.
- The customer feedback data needs to be aggregated for each customer.

sections.title: External APIs
sections.text:
The APIs provided by the delivery companies have the following capabilities and limitations:
- CompanyA's API has an OpenAPI definition.
- CompanyB's API does not have an OpenAPI definition.
- CompanyC's API has an OpenAPI definition and provides different API endpoints for TEST and PRODUCTION.
- All APIs use API keys for authentication.

questions.q: You need to create a custom connector that retrieves data from the API of CompanyA. Which two elements should you configure? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.
questions.why: CompanyA's API already has an OpenAPI definition, so you import/configure the connector from that OpenAPI definition, and you configure the Authentication model as API key (all APIs use API keys). 'Delivery reference' and 'Delivery parameter' are not custom-connector configuration elements.

questions.q: You need to retrieve customer feedback by creating an Azure Function using the JSON format. Which two actions should you perform? Each correct answer presents part of the solution.
options: Develop an API definition for the Azure Function.
Create a custom connector that works with JSON syntax.
Copy the JSON syntax to your application.
Import the necessary data by developing a Power Automate cloud flow.
Import a RESTful API hosted in Azure Logic Apps.
answer: Develop an API definition for the Azure Function.
Import a RESTful API hosted in Azure Logic Apps.
questions.why: To surface the Azure Function to Power Platform you develop an API definition (OpenAPI) for the function so a custom connector can consume it, and you import the RESTful API (exposed/hosted via Azure Logic Apps) to build the connector. Copying JSON to the app, creating a JSON-syntax connector by hand, or importing data with a Power Automate flow are not the correct steps to expose the Azure Function's feedback data.

questions.q: You need to enable CompanyC's custom connector to use different URL endpoints in the TEST and PRODUCTION environments. What should you use when configuring the custom connector?
questions.why: Use a Policy template when configuring the custom connector. A policy template defines values applied when a connection is made - specifically the 'Set Host URL' policy lets the same connector point at a different host/endpoint (TEST vs PRODUCTION) depending on the environment. A Postman collection or OpenAPI definition only describes the API, and an environment variable is a solution setting, not a connector-level mechanism to swap the host URL.

questions.q: You need to create the custom connector for CompanyB's delivery service. What should you use?
questions.why: CompanyB's API does NOT have an OpenAPI definition, so you cannot import one. Use Postman to send requests to CompanyB's API, save the requests/responses into a Postman collection, and then create the custom connector by importing that Postman collection. Azure AD is for authentication, and Azure Function/App Service are hosting services - none of them build the connector from an API that lacks an OpenAPI definition.
